title: SPICED: Sample-Efficient Prior-Informed Causal Estimation via Directed Information
description: A hybrid causal discovery algorithm that combines information-theoretic skeleton discovery (using k-NN entropy estimation) with continuous optimization guided by structural sparsity constraints. SPICED addresses the critical gap of sample-efficient causal discovery by leveraging directed information measures that are more robust than conditional independence tests with limited samples, while maintaining scalability through differentiable optimization. The method incorporates fixed-parameter tractability results by exploiting structural parameters (edge-connectivity, feedback vertex set) to guide and constrain the optimization, providing theoretical guarantees for sparse graphs.
motivation: Current causal discovery methods require large sample sizes: constraint-based methods (PC, FCI) need extensive data for reliable conditional independence testing and have exponential complexity, while differentiable methods (NOTEARS, GOLEM) require large samples for accurate gradient estimates. Recent sample-efficient approaches are either limited to specific model classes (LoSAM for ANMs), require quantum hardware (qPC), or need pre-training on synthetic data (SPOT). There is no method that combines sample efficiency, scalability, and applicability to general functional forms without specialized hardware or pre-training.
proposed_approach: Three-phase architecture: (1) Information-theoretic skeleton discovery using k-NN entropy estimators to robustly detect causal dependencies with small samples; (2) Structural constraint extraction analyzing edge-connectivity and feedback vertex set to obtain FPT guarantees; (3) Constrained continuous optimization combining NOTEARS-style acyclicity constraints with structural penalties and information-theoretic initialization. The key innovation is using adaptive information-theoretic scoring (more sample-efficient than CI testing) to guide differentiable optimization (more scalable than constraint-based search), while structural constraints prevent local minima.

related_work.title: DAGs with NO TEARS: Continuous optimization for structure learning
related_work.key_difference: NOTEARS requires large samples for gradient accuracy and lacks finite-sample guarantees. SPICED adds information-theoretic initialization and structural constraints for sample efficiency.

related_work.title: Causation, Prediction, and Search
related_work.key_difference: PC/FCI are statistically consistent but need exponential CI tests with low power for small samples. SPICED replaces exponential testing with polynomial optimization guided by information-theoretic screening.

related_work.title: Scalable differentiable causal discovery with latent confounders (SPOT)
related_work.key_difference: SPOT requires supervised pre-training on synthetic data. SPICED works out-of-the-box without pre-training.

related_work.title: Revisiting causal discovery from a complexity-theoretic perspective
related_work.key_difference: Provides FPT theory for constraint-based methods. SPICED is the first practical algorithm to incorporate these FPT results into differentiable causal discovery.

related_work.title: Local search in additive noise model (LoSAM)
related_work.key_difference: LoSAM achieves polynomial time but only for ANMs. SPICED handles general functional forms without parametric assumptions.

related_work.title: Quantum-enhanced causal discovery for small samples (qPC)
related_work.key_difference: Requires quantum hardware. SPICED runs on standard CPUs.

hypothesis: A hybrid algorithm using adaptive information-theoretic edge scoring (k-NN entropy estimation) combined with continuous optimization guided by structural sparsity constraints (edge-connectivity, FVS) will achieve superior sample efficiency compared to both pure constraint-based and pure differentiable methods for N < 500, while maintaining polynomial-time complexity for sparse graphs.

primary: SPICED achieves significantly lower SHD than NOTEARS for N <= 200 on at least 3 of 4 graph types (linear Gaussian, linear non-Gaussian, nonlinear, ANM)
SPICED runs in < 5 minutes for 50-node graphs on 2 CPU cores
On Sachs dataset, SPICED achieves SHD < 10
secondary: Structural constraints improve convergence vs unconstrained baseline
Method works without modification across different data types
failure_modes: Dense graphs with high edge-connectivity may not benefit from structural constraints
Complex nonlinear interactions may require more sophisticated entropy estimators

feasibility.computational_resources: CPU-only (2 cores, 128GB RAM). No GPU required.
feasibility.why_feasible: Algorithm uses polynomial-time operations only: O(n^2 * N * log N) for information estimation, FPT in structural parameters for constraint extraction, and polynomial optimization. All components are embarrassingly parallel and require only standard linear algebra operations.

expected_contributions: First causal discovery method combining information-theoretic screening with differentiable optimization and FPT structural constraints
Practical algorithm for small-sample causal discovery without requiring pre-training, quantum hardware, or parametric assumptions
Demonstration that structural sparsity parameters (edge-connectivity, FVS) can be exploited in differentiable causal discovery
Empirical validation that k-NN entropy estimation outperforms CI testing for small N in causal discovery
Open-source implementation enabling causal discovery in scientific domains with limited data
